summary.intent: Test the --evaluator-url mechanism end-to-end by building an HTTP wrapper server, wiring it into the live integration orchestrator, and running a full RED-GREEN-OBSERVER cycle. Then create EXAMPLES.md documenting non-trivial evaluator patterns, and scan a checkpoint branch for secrets/PII.
summary.outcome: Successfully validated the full HTTP transport chain (CLI → httpx → evaluator_http_server → bash → JSON) with identical scores to --evaluator-command. Fixed a broken model name reference (gpt-5.1-mini → gpt-5.1) across 6 files. Created EXAMPLES.md with 4 worked evaluator examples and linked it from README. Secret scan of entire/checkpoints/v1 found 0 real secrets (30 false positives, all conversation UUIDs).

repo: HTTP evaluator protocol is POST {"candidate": "..."} → {"score": 0.85, ...}; CLI has a preflight check validating server availability before optimization starts
All ~16 existing HTTP evaluator tests use mocked httpx — no tests hit a real HTTP server prior to this session
skill_clarity.sh evaluator gives a stable ~0.8731 baseline for the generate-evaluator SKILL.md artifact; artifact is near-ceiling so GREEN phase optimization finds no improvement
gpt-5.1-mini does not exist as an OpenAI model; gpt-5.1 is the correct general-purpose model; gpt-5.1-codex-mini exists but rejects the temperature parameter
entire/checkpoints/v1 branch contains 64 checkpoint commits and 313 files, mostly .jsonl session transcripts — UUIDs in these files trigger false positives in trufflehog's NpmToken detector
evaluation_pattern field documents 4 patterns (verification, judge, simulation, composite) but only judge had a real example before EXAMPLES.md

code.finding: New file. Content-Length must use len(bytes_body) not len(str_body) to avoid off-by-one errors with non-ASCII content. server_close() must be in a finally block to prevent socket leaks on unexpected exceptions.

Added --evaluator-url flag with mutual exclusion against --evaluator-command. Subprocess output parsing in the RED phase can lose JSON recovery when litellm debug output mixes with stdout, masking judge errors.
Help text example referenced gpt-5.1-mini (non-existent model); corrected to gpt-5.1.
7 occurrences of gpt-5.1-mini in judge model mocks; all corrected to gpt-5.1.

workflow: Subagent-driven development pattern: dispatch implementer → spec reviewer → code quality reviewer → apply fixes → run tests → commit is effective for medium-complexity tasks
trufflehog (brew install) is the best available local secret scanner; run with --only-verified first for signal, then without for completeness
UUIDs in transcript/log files will generate high volumes of false positives in npm token detectors — expected noise, not real findings
Testing with --only-verified + targeted grep for real patterns (sk-, AKIA, ghp_, private key headers, emails) gives a complete picture faster than raw trufflehog output

friction: gpt-5.1-mini was used as a model name in CLI help text, CLAUDE.md, live_integration.py, and tests — it doesn't exist, causing null scores in the RED phase and requiring a second fix pass when gpt-5.1-codex-mini also failed (no temperature support)

live_integration.py subprocess output parsing loses litellm's built-in JSON recovery (markdown code fence stripping) since stderr mixes with stdout, making judge errors look like invalid JSON rather than surfacing the real cause
trufflehog generated 30 false positives from conversation UUIDs matching the NpmToken regex, requiring manual verification of each finding
open_items: live_integration.py RED phase subprocess JSON parsing is fragile — should capture stderr separately or use litellm directly rather than shelling out, to preserve JSON recovery logic
EXAMPLES.md examples are illustrative but not runnable as-is (no companion script files) — consider adding scripts/examples/ directory with the actual evaluator code from each example
Model-id validator utility suggested to catch invalid litellm model names at startup rather than at inference time
No unit tests added for the new --evaluator-url path in live_integration.py — only covered by the live integration run
